content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 19, 1991


Penny Harding
Capital Advocates, Inc.
1127 11th Street, Suite 950
Sacramento, CA  98514


Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-91-302

Dear Ms. Harding:

     This is in reply to your letter requesting advice concerning
the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ Because
you have not identified the lobbying firms whose disclosure
obligations are in question, and have not indicated that you are
authorized to seek advice on their behalf, we are treating your
letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c) (copy enclosed).2/

QUESTION

     May a lobbying firm lobby on a pro bono basis on behalf of
the clients of Capital Advocates without amending its registration
statement to add Capital Advocates as a client?

CONCLUSION

     A lobbying firm may provide pro bono lobbying services for
Capital Advocates without amending its lobbying firm registration
statement.


_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000–91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Penny Harding
Page 2


FACTS

Capital Advocates, Inc. is a registered lobbying firm. With
the death of Capital Advocates lobbyist Bernard Teitelbaum, other
lobbying firms have offered to provide pro bono lobbying services
on behalf of the clients of Capital Advocates.

ANALYSIS

A lobbying firm is defined as:

...any business entity, including an individual contract
lobbyist, which...receives or becomes entitled to receive any
compensation, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel
expenses, for the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action on behalf of any other person, and any
partner, owner, officer or employee of the business entity is
a lobbyist.

Section 82038.5(a)(1).

Normally, when a lobbying firm subcontracts to lobby on
behalf of another lobbying firm's clients, the subcontracting firm
must amend its lobbying firm registration statement to add the
other lobbying firm as a client. However, as long as Capital
Advocates does not compensate the subcontracting firm (other than
reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses), the subcontracting
firm will not be required to amend its registration statement to
add Capital Advocates as a client.

However, Regulation 18613 (copy enclosed) requires a lobbying
firm to disclose on its quarterly disclosure report (Form 625) all
payments which benefit an elected state official, legislative
official, agency official, state candidate, or member of the
immediate family of one of these individuals ("activity
expenses"). Therefore, the subcontracting firm will be required
to disclose on its Form 625 any activity expenses it incurs or
pays on behalf of Capital Advocates or its clients. Please note
that reimbursement by Capital Advocates to the subcontracting firm
for activity expenses or any other business expenses (other than
reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses) will require the
subcontracting firm to amend its registration to add Capital
Advocates as a client. (Richardson Advice Letter, No. A-90-431,
copy enclosed.)

Penny Harding
Page 3


If you have any questions or need further assistance, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                                Sincerely,

                                                Scott Hallabrin
                                                Acting General Counsel

                                                [signature]

                                                by: Colleen McGee
                                                    Political Reform Consultant
                                                    Technical Assistance and
                                                    Analysis Division

Enclosures

sections.question: May a lobbying firm lobby on a pro bono basis on behalf of the clients of Capital Advocates without amending its registration statement to add Capital Advocates as a client?
sections.conclusion:
A lobbying firm may provide pro bono lobbying services for Capital Advocates without amending its lobbying firm registration statement.

1/ Government Code Sections §1000 - §1015. , et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

sections.analysis:
A lobbying firm is defined as:

...any business entity, including an individual contract lobbyist, which...receives or becomes entitled to receive any compensation, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on behalf of any other person, and any partner, owner, officer or employee of the business entity is a lobbyist.

Section 82038.5(a)(1).

Normally, when a lobbying firm subcontracts to lobby on behalf of another lobbying firm's clients, the subcontracting firm must amend its lobbying firm registration statement to add the other lobbying firm as a client. However, as long as Capital Advocates does not compensate the subcontracting firm (other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses), the subcontracting firm will not be required to amend its registration statement to add Capital Advocates as a client.

However, Regulation 18613 (copy enclosed) requires a lobbying firm to disclose on its quarterly disclosure report (Form 625) all payments which benefit an elected state official, legislative official, agency official, state candidate, or member of the immediate family of one of these individuals ("activity expenses"). Therefore, the subcontracting firm will be required to disclose on its Form 625 any activity expenses it incurs or pays on behalf of Capital Advocates or its clients. Please note that reimbursement by Capital Advocates to the subcontracting firm for activity expenses or any other business expenses (other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses) will require the subcontracting firm to amend its registration to add Capital Advocates as a client. (Richardson Advice Letter, No. A-90-431, copy enclosed.)
